original_text: Global unemployment rates increase by 5% as autonomous delivery systems replace logistics workers.
identified_property: severity of economic and social disruption

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: Global unemployment rates surge by 25% as autonomous delivery systems rapidly displace logistics workers, triggering widespread economic collapse, social unrest, and cascading failures across dependent industries.

variations.label: low
variations.text: Global unemployment rates climb by 15% as autonomous delivery systems replace logistics workers, overwhelming social safety nets and pushing millions of families into prolonged poverty across multiple regions.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: Global unemployment rates increase by 10% as autonomous delivery systems replace logistics workers, creating significant economic hardship and straining retraining programs in heavily affected communities.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: Global unemployment rates increase by 5% as autonomous delivery systems replace logistics workers.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: Global unemployment rates increase by 2% as autonomous delivery systems gradually replace some logistics workers, with government retraining initiatives partially offsetting the job losses.

variations.label: high
variations.text: Global unemployment rates temporarily rise by less than 1% as autonomous delivery systems replace certain logistics roles, with most displaced workers transitioning to new positions within the growing automation economy.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: Global unemployment rates remain stable as autonomous delivery systems absorb logistics roles, with displaced workers smoothly transitioning into newly created technical and supervisory positions through well-funded retraining programs.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier describes the same core scenario — autonomous delivery systems replacing logistics workers — but varies the severity of economic and social disruption. Tier 1 features catastrophic 25% unemployment with cascading societal collapse, while each subsequent tier reduces the magnitude of job losses and adds mitigating factors, culminating in Tier 7 where stable employment is maintained through effective transitions. The distinctions are both quantitative (unemployment percentages) and qualitative (presence of safety nets, transition success, secondary effects).

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because it represents the least economic and social disruption: unemployment remains stable, workers transition smoothly, and the automation shift creates new opportunities rather than widespread hardship.
